revision.comment: Auto-generated page using script written by Immanuelle, either as a placeholder redirect or to make bots recognize the page as made by Immanuelle
revision.text: #redirect[[en:Twelve-stage_Bugaku]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

'''天宮神社の十二段舞楽'''（あめのみやじんじゃのじゅうにだんぶがく）は、[[静岡県]][[周智郡]][[森町 (静岡県)|森町]]天宮に伝わる民俗芸能の[[舞楽]]。
[[1976年]][[12月25日]]付けで[[選択無形民俗文化財]]、[[1982年]][[1月14日]]には[[小国神社の舞楽]]および[[山名神社天王祭舞楽]]とともに[[遠江森町の舞楽]]として[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
{{japan-culture-stub}} [[category:静岡県|あめのみやしんしやのしゆうにたんふかく]] [[Category:伝統芸能|あめのみやしんしやのしゆうにたんふかく]]

revision.text:
'''天宮神社の十二段舞楽'''（あめのみやじんじゃのじゅうにだんぶがく）は、[[静岡県]][[周智郡]][[森町 (静岡県)|森町]]天宮に伝わる民俗芸能の[[舞楽]]。
[[1976年]][[12月25日]]付けで[[選択無形民俗文化財]]、[[1982年]][[1月14日]]には[[小国神社の舞楽]]および[[山名神社天王祭舞楽]]とともに[[遠江森町の舞楽]]として[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
{{japan-culture-stub}} [[Category:静岡県の祭り|あめのみやしんしやのしゆうにたんふかく]] [[Category:重要無形民俗文化財|あめのみやしんしやのしうにたんふかく]] [[Category:森町_(静岡県)|あめのみやしんしやのしうにたんふかく]]

'''天宮神社の十二段舞楽'''（あめのみやじんじゃのじゅうにだんぶがく）は、[[静岡県]][[周智郡]][[森町 (静岡県)|森町]]天宮に伝わる民俗芸能の[[舞楽]]。[[東海地方]]に[[伝承]]されている地方化された舞楽の一つで、4月の第1土曜日と日曜日に演じられる。
[[天宮神社]]の拝殿前にある高欄つきの舞楽殿が舞台となり、舞人は稚児4人、15歳前後の少年、大人11人で、囃子には大太鼓・鉦・鼓・笛が付く。曲目は「延舞」「色香」「庭胡蝶」「鳥名」「太平楽」「新靺鞨」「安摩」「二の舞」「陵王」「抜頭」「納曽利」「獅子」の12曲である。
[[1976年]]（[[昭和]]51年）[[12月25日]][[選択無形民俗文化財|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財]]に選択され、[[1982年]]（昭和57年）[[1月14日]]には、[[山名神社天王祭舞楽]]および[[小国神社の舞楽]]とともに[[遠江森町の舞楽]]として[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
{{japan-culture-stub}} [[Category:静岡県の祭り|あめのみやしんしやのしゆうにたんふかく]] [[Category:重要無形民俗文化財|あめのみやしんしやのしうにたんふかく]] [[Category:森町_(静岡県)|あめのみやしんしやのしうにたんふかく]] [[Category:雅楽|あめのみやしんしやのしうにたんふかく]]

[[天宮神社]]の拝殿前にある高欄つきの舞楽殿が舞台となり、舞人は稚児4人、15歳前後の少年、大人11人で、囃子には大太鼓・鉦・鼓・笛が付く。曲目は「延舞」「色香」「庭胡蝶」「鳥名」「太平楽」「新靺鞨」「安摩」「二の舞」「陵王」「抜頭」「納曽利」「獅子」の12曲である。
[[1976年]]（[[昭和]]51年）[[12月25日]][[選択無形民俗文化財|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財]]に選択され、[[1982年]]（昭和57年）[[1月14日]]には、[[山名神社天王祭舞楽]]および[[小国神社の舞楽]]とともに[[遠江森町の舞楽]]として[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
{{japan-culture-stub}} [[Category:静岡県の祭り|あめのみやしんしやのしゆうにたんふかく]] [[Category:無形民俗文化財|あめのみやしんしやのしうにたんふかく]] [[Category:森町_(静岡県)|あめのみやしんしやのしうにたんふかく]] [[Category:雅楽|あめのみやしんしやのしうにたんふかく]]

[[天宮神社]]の拝殿前にある高欄つきの舞楽殿が舞台となり、舞人は稚児4人、15歳前後の少年、大人11人で、囃子には大太鼓・鉦・鼓・笛が付く。曲目は「延舞」「色香」「庭胡蝶」「鳥名」「太平楽」「新靺鞨」「安摩」「二の舞」「陵王」「抜頭」「納曽利」「獅子」の12曲である。

revision.text:
天宮神社の十二段舞楽
[[Category:天宮神社の十二段舞楽]][[ja:天宮神社の十二段舞楽]]

'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).
{{Japan-culture-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Amemiya Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku}} [[Category:Festivals in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Morimachi, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Gagaku]] [[Category:Intangible Folk Cultural Properties in need of measures such as recording]]
[[Category:天宮神社の十二段舞楽]][[ja:天宮神社の十二段舞楽]]

'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

revision.text:
'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社}}. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

revision.text:
'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

revision.text:
'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

revision.text:
'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社|1=Ame-no-Miya Shrine}}. On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. revision.text:
'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社|1=Ame-no-Miya Shrine}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社|1=Ame-no-Miya Shrine}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|ja|周智郡}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社|1=Ame-no-Miya Shrine}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).
On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982年1月14日}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

revision.text:
'''天宮神社の十二段舞楽'''（あめのみやじんじゃのじゅうにだんぶがく）は、[[静岡県]][[周智郡]][[森町 (静岡県)|森町]]天宮に伝わる民俗芸能の[[舞楽]]。[[東海地方]]に[[伝承]]されている地方化された舞楽の一つで、4月の第1土曜日と日曜日に演じられる。
[[天宮神社]]の拝殿前にある高欄つきの舞楽殿が舞台となり、舞人は稚児4人、15歳前後の少年、大人11人で、囃子には大太鼓・鉦・鼓・笛が付く。曲目は「延舞」「色香」「庭胡蝶」「鳥名」「太平楽」「新靺鞨」「安摩」「二の舞」「陵王」「抜頭」「納曽利」「獅子」の12曲である。
[[1976年]]（[[昭和]]51年）[[12月25日]][[記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財]]に選択され、[[1982年]]（昭和57年）[[1月14日]]には、[[山名神社天王祭舞楽]]および[[小国神社の舞楽]]とともに[[遠江森町の舞楽]]として[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
{{japan-culture-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:あめのみやしんしやのしゆうにたんふかく}} [[Category:静岡県の祭り]] [[Category:静岡県森町の歴史]] [[Category:雅楽]] [[Category:記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財]]

It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]], performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.

The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).

The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).

On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 25, 1976|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|qq=}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982nd year 1月14日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 14, 1982|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|qq=}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|1=Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459369}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]], performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.

The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bugaku Hall|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, featuring railings in front of the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社|1=Ame-no-Miya Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengū Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Miya Shrine|qq=}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).

On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 25, 1976|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|qq=}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982nd year 1月14日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 14, 1982|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|qq=}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|1=Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459369}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11442539]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bugaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 舞|1=mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109903880}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Morimachi, Shizuoka|1=Morimachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q854613}}, [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], specifically in the Tengū area of [[:en:Shūchi District, Shizuoka|Shūchi District, Shizuoka]]. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]], performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.

The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bugaku Hall|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, featuring railings in front of the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社|1=Ame-no-Miya Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengū Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Miya Shrine|qq=}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).

On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 25, 1976|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|qq=}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982nd year 1月14日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 14, 1982|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|qq=}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|1=Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459369}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bugaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 舞|1=mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109903880}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Morimachi, Shizuoka|1=Morimachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q854613}}, [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], specifically in the Tengū area of [[:en:Shūchi District, Shizuoka|Shūchi District, Shizuoka]]. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]], performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.

The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bugaku Hall|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, featuring railings in front of the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社|1=Ame-no-Miya Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengū Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Miya Shrine|qq=}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982nd year 1月14日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 14, 1982|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|qq=}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|1=Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459369}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bugaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 舞|1=mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109903880}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Morimachi, Shizuoka|1=Morimachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q854613}}, [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], specifically in the Tengū area of [[:en:Shūchi District, Shizuoka|Shūchi District, Shizuoka]]. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]], performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.

The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bugaku Hall|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, featuring railings in front of the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社|1=Ame-no-Miya Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengū Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Miya Shrine|qq=}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).

On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 25, 1976|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|qq=}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982nd year 1月14日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 14, 1982|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|qq=}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|1=Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459369}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11442539]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Tengū Shrine’s Twelve-Step Bugaku''' (天宮神社の十二段舞楽, Amemiya Jinja no Jūnidangaku) is a traditional folk performing art of {{ill|Bugaku|ja|舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bugaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 舞|1=mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109903880}} passed down in {{ill|Morimachi, Shizuoka|ja|森町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Morimachi, Shizuoka|1=Morimachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q854613}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, specifically in the Tengū area of {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|en|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|WD=Q1205501|lt=Shūchi District, Shizuoka}}. It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.

The performance takes place on the stage of the {{ill|Bugaku Hall|ja|舞楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bugaku Hall|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, featuring railings in front of the {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3125745|lt=Haiden}} of the {{ill|Tengū Shrine|ja|天宮神社|1=Ame-no-Miya Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengū Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Miya Shrine|qq=}}. The dancers include four children, boys around 15 years old, and 11 adults. Accompaniment is provided by large drums, gongs, small drums, and flutes. The repertoire consists of 12 pieces: "Enbu" (延舞), "Iroka" (色香), "Niwa Chō" (庭胡蝶), "Tori-na" (鳥名), "Taihei-raku" (太平楽), "Shin Makka" (新靺鞨), "Ama" (安摩), "Ni no Mai" (二の舞), "Ryōō" (陵王), "Bato" (抜頭), "Nasu" (納曽利), and "Shishi" (獅子).

On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 25, 1976|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|qq=}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982nd year 1月14日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 14, 1982|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|qq=}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|1=Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459369}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

On {{ill|December 25, 1976|ja|1976年12月25日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 25, 1976|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was selected as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property in need of measures such as recording|qq=}}. Later, on {{ill|January 14, 1982|ja|1982nd year 1月14日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 14, 1982|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} alongside the {{ill|Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|ja|山名神社天王祭舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamana Shrine Tenno Festival Bugaku|qq=}} and the {{ill|Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|ja|小国神社の舞楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|1=Oguni Shrine’s Bugaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459369}} under the collective name "Bugaku of Morimachi in Tōtōmi" (遠江森町の舞楽).

It is one of the regionalized forms of Bugaku handed down in the {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region|1=Tōkai region}}, performed annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of April.
